articles.title: IMD issues heatwave alert for Rayalaseema as temperature soars to 43°C - The Times of India
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andhra Pradesh
articles.full_text:
Visakhapatnam: The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a heatwave alert for parts of the Rayalaseema region from April 15 to 18, warning of a gradual rise in maximum temperatures across the state over the coming days.
The Rayalaseema region in Andhra Pradesh is in the grip of scorching temperatures, with mercury levels soaring past 43 degrees Celsius in certain parts of the region on Tuesday. High temperatures and high relative humidity were recorded in a few parts of the coastal Andhra Pradesh (CAP) region.
Anantapur in the Rayalaseema region emerged as the hottest location in the state on Tuesday (April 14), recording a maximum temperature of 43.1°C. Kurnool closely followed at 42.5°C, Kadapa at 42°C, Nandyal at 41.2°C, and Tirupati at 40.4°C. In the CAP region, Jangamaheswarapram in Palnadu district recorded 41°C, followed by 39.2°C at Nandigama, Gannavaram, and Amaravati, while the remaining parts of the state saw maximum temperatures between 34°C and 38°C. The lowest maximum temperature recorded in the state was 32.7°C in Kalingapatnam of Srikakulam district.
The weathermen said hot and humid weather conditions are likely to occur at isolated places over CAP from April 15 to 20. As per IMD, the maximum temperatures are expected to increase by 2°C to 3°C in parts of the state over the next three days.
Like the rest of the country, most parts of the Rayalaseema and CAP in the state are expected to receive below-normal rainfall during the southwest monsoon between June and September in 2026 amid the growing risk of the El Niño phenomenon.
Weather officials said no significant rainfall is expected in the state for the next week. With hot and humid conditions, people will experience discomfort. With temperatures soaring across the state, health authorities issued an advisory urging residents to exercise extreme caution to prevent sunstroke and heat-related illnesses.
